## *PLAN 6068 AI Research Proposal: Extracting Rooftop Solar "Panel Row" Features from Remote Sensing Imagery and Building Footprint Polygons*

##### **Alejandro S. Vega Nogales; Prof. J. Ayala Hernández**

### **1\. Research Problem**

#### **Local Context**

Following the catastrophic electrical grid failures experienced during Hurricanes Maria and Fiona, Puerto Rico has undergone a rapid and largely uncoordinated proliferation of decentralized rooftop photovoltaic (PV) solar panel installations. Driven by a necessity for energy resilience against an unreliable, centralized electrical grid, the number of grid-connected PV systems installed by residents and businesses increased nearly tenfold between 2017 and 2024 (NEPR, 2024). By June 2025, approximately 1.2 Gigawatts of capacity generated from over 117,000 net-metered residential and commercial rooftop PV installations accounted for more than 10% of Puerto Rico's total electricity consumption, up from 2% to 3% before Hurricane Maria (IEEFA, 2024, 2025). This private citizen-led shift toward distributed energy occurs against a backdrop of stagnant utility-scale progress (PR100 2023). Although the Puerto Rico Legislature established ambitious Renewable Portfolio Standards —most recently updated in 2019 to mandate 40% renewable energy by 2025 and 100% by 2050—the island has failed to meet every single interim milestone to date. (IEEFA, 2024).

#### **Problem Statement**

While this transition represents a significant move toward renewable energy, its decentralized nature presents a critical challenge for urban planners and grid operators. The vast majority of these installations represent the individual decisions of home and business owners rather than a coordinated policy-led infrastructure plan. Currently, both grid operators and urban planners lack a granular, validated spatial inventory of these distributed energy resources. For the former, the lack of an array-level geospatial inventory makes it very challenging to accurately produce short-term forecasts of site-level PV energy production or to balance localized loads effectively. For the latter, the lack of mapped data prevents identifying and addressing significant "energy justice" gaps (Peña-Becerra et al., 2025). High upfront costs remain a barrier for the majority of the population leading to a deployment outlook in 2023 where 71% of systems are owned by high-income households (IEEFA, 2024).  
The use of Computer Vision AI/ML methodologies for the identification of installed solar panels has been an active area of research for almost a decade. Existing models like those developed by *Kruitwagen et al. (2021)* and *Robinson et al. (2025)* are trained on datasets of large, utility-scale solar farms. These models face significant challenges when applied to the detection of the smaller, residential installations in the tropical and visually dense urban environments of PR. As identified by *Hu et al.* (2024), most published work on the topic often suffer from **four critical pitfalls: distribution shift, poor test data quality, inappropriate spatial aggregation, and proprietary data**. There is an addressable need to move beyond broad regional estimates and address the characteristics of contexts where these smaller, distributed installations dominate the energy market.

#### **Research Questions**

To bridge the gap between high-level macro-estimations and the granular needs of local grid planning and energy justice assessment, this research proposes the following questions:

1. Can we **successfully reproduce** —**in the rooftop PV context**— the **superpixel segmentation methodology** established by *Stid et al. (2025)* for transforming coarse Solar Array installation polygons into granular "panel-row" rectangles that outline each row of solar panels in the installation?  
2. *Is there **a statistically-significant spatial correlation between** the presence or installation-rate-per-housing-units of **rooftop PV systems and sociodemographic indicators from the US 2020 Census** Tracts for Puerto Rico?*  
3. To what extent **are geophysical variables** that measure Solar Irradiance, Ground Temperature, and Land Cover **correlated to the presence of residential PV solar** compared to socioeconomic factors?

### **2\. Data to be Used**

* **Building Footprints:** [Overture Maps building footprints](https://docs.overturemaps.org/guides/buildings/) for Puerto Rico to define the search space for rooftop installations and validated against [CRIM cadastres](https://catastro.crimpr.net/cdprpc/).  
* **LUMA/**Negociado de Energía de PR (**NEPR**) [Interconnection data](https://energia.pr.gov/en/dockets/?docket=nepr-mi-2019-0016)   
* **PV Installation Vectors:** [Publicly available solar panel installation geometries](https://www.google.com/search?q=https://www.linkedin.com/posts/activity-7419870346658340864-w4DW?utm_source%3Dsocial_share_send%26utm_medium%3Dmember_desktop_web%26rcm%3DACoAABzlSsgBeKUQFer4yf0gRV-2YDbDHjq4J4Y) from OpenStreetMap (OSM) to serve as rooftops to analyze and as validation data.  
* **Sociodemographic Data:** US Census 2020 (Tract level) data for Puerto Rico, focusing on household income, education, race, and other sociodemographic variables.  
* **Geophysical Variables:** Solar irradiance and ground temperature from the [NREL’s National Solar Radiation Database (NSRDB)](https://www.nlr.gov/hpc/nsrdb-dataset), and Land Cover data [from Overture Maps](https://docs.overturemaps.org/blog/2024/05/16/land-cover/) derived from ESA’s 10m WorldCover rasters.  
* **High-Resolution Imagery:** Multispectral satellite and aerial imagery providing the *sub-meter resolution* necessary for panel-row segmentation from several sources:  
  * Images from [Vantor/Maxar Open Access Program](https://github.com/opengeos/maxar-open-data)  
  * [Earthview dataset](https://satellogic-earthview.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/index.html) from Satellogic  
  * [NAIP STAC catalog](https://stac.digitalforestry.org/collections/naip?.language=en)  
  * Paid Imagery from [Google Solar API](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/solar/coverage) 

![][image1]

### **3\. Methodology and AI Tool(s)**

This project utilizes a sophisticated stack of geospatial datasets, data engineering pipelines, and “agentic” AI coding assistants to accelerate the development of jupyter notebooks (published in and shared via Google Colab).

#### **Python Libraries and Geospatial Analysis Development Stack**

| Category | Libraries |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Geospatial Processing & Data** | [GeoPandas](https://geopandas.org/en/stable/getting_started/introduction.html), [Shapely](https://shapely.readthedocs.io/en/stable/manual.html), [DuckDB](https://duckdb.org/2025/08/08/spatial-joins), [censusdis](https://censusdis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/nb/Data%20With%20Geometry.html),  |
| **Raster Analysis** | [Rasterio](https://rasterio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/intro.html), [Xarray](https://docs.xarray.dev/en/stable/getting-started-guide/why-xarray.html), [rasteret](https://terrafloww.github.io/rasteret/getting-started/#browse-the-catalog) |
| **Computer Vision & AI** | [scikit-image](https://mpolinowski.github.io/docs/Development/Python/2023-07-09-python-scikit-image-intro/2023-07-09/), [GeoAI](https://opengeoai.org/#introduction), [earthengine-api](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/guides/python_install),  |
| **Visualization** | [Seaborn](https://seaborn.pydata.org/tutorial/introduction.html), [Folium](https://realpython.com/python-folium-web-maps-from-data/), [lonboard](https://docs.overturemaps.org/examples/lonboard/), [stac-map](https://developmentseed.org/blog/2025-09-02-stacmap/) |

#### **AI Developer Assistant Tool**

The implementation phase leverages the [GitHub Copilot Coding Agent](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/concepts/agents/coding-agent/about-coding-agent) within VS Code to accelerate the development of complex geospatial processing scripts and visualization logic. We will use coding focused LLM models such as ChatGPT Codex, and Claude Opus. By providing high-level natural language descriptions of desired features, the tool assists in generating lengthy boilerplate code for imagery ingestion and geometric transformations. This is particularly valuable for leveraging established geospatial development patterns and implementing visualization tools and interactive GUI elements.

### **4\. Expected Results**

As this is a course-based project developed over an approx. two-month period, the outputs serve as a development prototype and planning proof-of-concept for regional PV assessment:

* **Prototype Vector Dataset:** A sample geospatial dataset produced via the superpixel segmentation and supervised classification methods, providing granular "panel-row" geometries for selected focus areas in Puerto Rico. This output demonstrates the feasibility of moving from coarse installation points to detailed array footprints.  
* **Performance Benchmark Table:** A table evaluating the proposed automated method against known ground-truth installations. This assessment will quantify the accuracy of the prototype in identifying residential PV arrays within tropical urban environments.  
* **Correlation Analysis:** An exploratory data analysis (EDA) exploring the spatial correlation between identified installation locations and corresponding sociodemographic and geophysical variables (e.g., income levels and solar irradiance). This analysis provides an initial look at how planners can eventually evaluate both energy equity disparities and deployment efficiency at scale.

## **References**

1. J. T. Stid et al, *“A harmonized dataset of ground-mounted solar energy in the US with enhanced metadata*” Nature Scientific Data, vol. 12, no. 1, p. 1586, Sep. 2025, doi: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-025-05862-4](https://www.google.com/search?q=https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-025-05862-4).  
2. W. Hu et al., ‘*What you get is not always what you see—pitfalls in solar array assessment using overhead imagery*’, Applied Energy, vol. 327, p. 120143, Dec. 2022, doi: [10.1016/j.apenergy.	2022.120143](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apenergy.2022.120143).  
3. Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis, *‘Solar at a crossroads in Puerto Rico*’. Available: [https://ieefa.org/resources/solar-crossroads-puerto-rico](https://ieefa.org/resources/solar-crossroads-puerto-rico)  
4. IEEFA, *‘Rooftop Solar in Puerto Rico Reaches 10%, Grid Reliability Continues to Wane‘*. Available: [https://ieefa.org/resources/rooftop-solar-puerto-rico-reaches-10-grid-reliability-continues-wane](https://ieefa.org/resources/rooftop-solar-puerto-rico-reaches-10-grid-reliability-continues-wane)  
5. C. A. Peña-Becerra et al, *‘Barrio-Level Assessment of Solar Rooftop Energy and Initial Insights into Energy Inequalities in Puerto Rico*’, Solar, vol. 5, no. 2, p. 28, Jun. 2025, doi: [10.3390/solar5020028](https://www.google.com/search?q=https://doi.org/10.3390/solar5020028).  
6. L. Kruitwagen et al, *‘A global inventory of photovoltaic solar energy generating units’*, Nature, vol. 598, no. 7882, pp. 604–610, Oct. 2021, doi: [10.1038/s41586-021-03957-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-03957-7).  
7. S. He et al, ‘*Developing a Universal Spectral Index for Solar Photovoltaic Panels: A Methodology for Spatial Information Extraction from Satellite Remote Sensing Imagery*’, 2024\. doi: [10.2139/ssrn.4855811](https://www.google.com/search?q=https://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.4855811).  
8. C. Robinson et al., ‘*Global Renewables Watch: A Temporal Dataset of Solar and Wind Energy Derived from Satellite Imagery*’, 2025, arXiv. doi: [10.48550/ARXIV.2503.14860](https://www.google.com/search?q=https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2503.14860).  
9. J. Yu et al, ‘*DeepSolar: A Machine Learning Framework to Efficiently Construct a Solar Deployment Database in the United States*’, Joule, vol. 2, no. 12, pp. 2605–2617, Dec. 2018, doi: [10.1016/j.joule.2018.11.021](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joule.2018.11.021)  
10. M. Baggu et al., ‘Puerto Rico Grid Resilience and Transitions to 100% Renewable Energy Study (PR100): Final Report’, NREL/TP--6A20-88384, 2335361, MainId:89161, Mar. 2024\. doi: [10.2172/2335361](https://www.google.com/search?q=https://doi.org/10.2172/2335361).
